<p></p><h1>HOW TO PROMOTE YOUR ART WITHOUT FEELING GROSS ABOUT IT</h1><p><br></p><blockquote><strong>"Artists make art for themselves. Legends make art for the people." - Bronques</strong></blockquote><p>So here's a simple formula. For every hour that you spend making art, you should put aside another hour to promote it. OR at least an hour trying to find the person who can help promote it for you. </p><p>Art is about communication. So when you don't promote your art, you're not finishing what you started. You should be your own number one fan and be relentlessly sharing your new magical concoction.</p><p>Like. You have an email list right? But what use is your email list if you're never gonna use it? </p><p>And your excuse it... You're afraid that people will get sick of hearing from you? Do you get sick of hearing from your favorite artists? </p><p><br></p><p><strong>FRIENDZONED</strong></p><p>To me it's just like when you’re afraid to making a move with a girl you like. Even though she's giving you all the signs. You know what's gonna happen right? You're gonna get friend-zoned. You’re gonna get “friend-zoned” with your own art. All because... You didn’t want to push too hard. All Because basically you’re chicken shit. Maybe your fans had a deep ass crush on you at one point but... you never made a move… So now you’ve kinda lost your edge. The other boy came in and stole your spot. Maybe he’s not "good for her", but at least he’s not afraid to tell her what he wants. That boy will tell it like it is. He wants more than “just talking”. He wants love. He wants a relationship. What do you want? You're worried about being nice. Stop being “nice” when your fans give you all the openings. Make a move. Don’t be friend zoned by never bringing up that you do this for a living. You can’t hang out with a girl for 3 months like you're friends and one day just go to kiss her out of the blue. You gotta tell her every day that you love her and what your intentions are. Let people know that you’re selling your art... or your services or whatever. Make it clear every step of the way. Otherwise eventually the world won't "see you that way" anymore. They'll move on. Because now they see you as a "9 to 5er" instead of an artist, a "lifer".</p><p><br></p><p><strong>PRICELESS</strong></p><p>Stop acting like your art is worthless... when it’s actually priceless. Don’t let them hang it on their walls for free. You know what I'm sayin'? Your art is valueable. but you treat it like it belongs in a bargain bin. So then of course... Others do the same.</p><p> </p><blockquote><strong> "It is irresponsible to bring your product or service to the marketplace without the same passion with which you created it." Brian Kurtz </strong></blockquote><p><br></p><p>Posting once on social media about your art isn't called "promotion". That's just called "checking a box". Where's your passion?</p><p>Where do you start? This might help.</p><p>You have to ask yourself one little question. Your art... What does it cure?</p><p>If you don’t feel like your art is the cure for someone’s affliction then of course you'll never be able to sell it. "What affliction is your art a cure for?" Will it make people laugh? Will it make them want to dance? Will it make them less lonely? Will it empower them by expanding their thinking? Will it make them see themselves in a new way Figure that out. Make your art the pill... for someone else's ills.</p><p>And once you’ve identified what your art is the cure for... that sentence you came up with will be your “pitch”. Like... I wrote something to help cure loneliness/I think this video I made is pretty funny you should watch it, it might brighten your day/ or "This is my most introspective song ever... can I send it to you?"</p><p><br></p><p><strong>YOU HAVE TO PRACTICE MAKING NOISE</strong></p><p>Being an artist isn't just about practicing your guitar or learning about cool photographic lighting... You need to practice sharing your work. Like every day of your career. </p><p>If you don't practice promoting yourself then when you'll be faced with an opportunity that can change your life you probably won't be ready. When the person that can catapult your career to new heights will walk in the room... That's not the day that you're gonna suddenly be "not shy". You won't know how to do it. You won't know what to say.</p><p>The kind of practice I'm telling you to engage in is what will make you certain that when you get presented with an opportunity one day that really matters... YOU DON'T CHOKE.</p><p><br></p><p><strong>WHY NO ONE ELSE CAN DO IT FOR YOU</strong></p><p>Often people tell me... as their main excuse for not promoting themselves... is that they're just waiting to get a manager for that.</p><p>But my question is always. And how exactly are you gonna get a manager if you don't promote yourself?</p><p>But you know why you're using this excuse?</p><p>People will do anything to not talk to other people. Even hurt themselves. Do you know why artists hate to promote their work? Because it’s the one time that they have to leave the nest. It’s the one time they have to get truly uncomfortable. They will do the least amount necessary just so that they can say they did something today. That’s the moment when we learn as the audience… those of us that are watching closely at least… that the uplifting things that these artists sing about, or that they write about, or that they depict in their photographs... it's just an aesthetic. It's all just for show. They don’t actually live with the courage and conviction they talk about. They’re hobbists. They don’t actually believe that their art holds a cure. They just wanna be famous like that other person from their hometown. ‘Cause when it comes to doing more than the obvious… nah. They’re chicken shit. But let's analyze that for a minute. What exactly are they afraid of? Beating on a few doors? The funniest thing is that when I talk about this... People always feel personally attacked. They literally think I’m talking about them. The truth is... It’s an epidemic. People are making art but they’re ashamed of promoting their work. They accuse the ones out there making noise-- of being too thirsty… But they’re the ones that are thirsty… They're thirsty for approval. That's why they conform. That’s why they don’t go for it. But the best excuse I've heard in my life is also the most tragic. It goes like this: “Do things at your own pace. Build it and they will come.” No babe. The only thing that will come is the realization… uh... when you’re still working a 9-5 a few years later… that those touchy-feely best-self quotes that you double-tap are straight up B.S.. It’s not about 10,000 hours! Gladwell forgot to mention the promotion part. 10 thousand hours will give you mastery. But it won’t make you KNOWN. </p><p><br></p><p><strong>MAYBE YOU JUST DON'T BELIEVE</strong></p><p>If you believed in your art, you would promote it. Oh... you tell me you believe in it but you’re too shy? Then you don’t believe. It's simple. When you believe… shyness doesn’t play into the equation. Fear doesn’t factor into the script. When you believe, you transcend adversity. Nothing can stop you. You beat doors down. You’re excited. Listen, even if you try to withdraw into your shell right now and convince yourself that I’m wrong and that I don’t understand your unique situation… It still won’t change the fact: You don’t believe. You'll find a counter-mantra to my self-empowerment mantra just to make yourself feel better. Words like "Everyone has their own path..." No. You're dabbling. Your 100% only happens 20% of the time. But when you believe, your 100% happens 100% of the time. If I can think of ways that you can be doing more… then you’re not doing enough. I’m not smarter than you. I’m just more relentless than you. Which is fine but... No one should be more relentless than you about your own shit. If I have to convince you to go harder then you don't deserve to play in this arena. I can always tell the people who don’t believe. They’re the ones that say that they can’t... instead of asking the real question. How. </p><p><br></p><p><strong>DROP BOMBS </strong></p><p>You need to drop bombs every day. You need to put things out into the world that people can interact with. If you're using social media, be social. Write captions to explain your work and put it into a context. Don't expect for your art to speak for itself. Talk about it like you would to your friends. Share it with the same intimacy you'd want your favorite artist to share their work with you.</p><p><br></p><p><strong>Photographers</strong></p><p>Photographers... Explain the passion behind your photography in your captions so that people can understand your worldview and what makes you different. Take pictures as if they're editorials. Don't be so disposable. When you go out into the world... Take portraits of the people who can influence your career and then email them the best photo of them that you took to open up a dialogue. </p><p><br></p><p><strong>Writers</strong></p><p>Writers... I know people have short attention spans. They don't read. Just share shorter bits. Don't be so precious. Put up some quotes on your social media instead of sharing other people's work. Flex your muscles in your captions. Text a friend a short paragraph and see what they think. Put a little bit of your writing as a signature at the bottom of your emails with a link to your medium page or your blog. You don't have a medium page or a blog? Well fuck... Get one. You grow when you write every day. But You grow even faster when you SHARE your writing every day.</p><p><br></p><p><strong>Musicians</strong></p><p>Musicians... Send your music to Instagram models. Give your music to film directors and to screenwriters. Make the perfect playlist for your local coffee shop and mix in your own songs with the others. Tell people why you wrote that song. Share the process of recording. Not like how other people do it, but in a way that's truly your own. Don't think in product cycles as if you're a record label. Use your freedom to overshare. That's what your friends want. That's what your fans need.</p><p><br></p><p><strong><em>My fellow artists... Promote yourselves guys. Your art deserves it.</em></strong></p><p></p>
</div>

    
  </div>

There are some great books out there on the psychology of sales (and I am saving some of them) , but what you are putting down here is a how to for artists the likes of which I have never seen. YES - this is scary as hell for me... but it makes emotional sense... I'll be back tomorrow after I post something - sharing one of the ways I practice harmonica, my thought process behind it, and how it feels to gain mastery of a pathway based on the major scale...</div>
